title: Transatlantic trade
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on transatlantic trade.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“[Hawkins] passed to Sierra Leone, upon the coast of Guinea . . . where he stayed some good time, and got into his possession, partly by the sword and partly by other means, to the number of 300 negroes at the least, besides other merchandises, which that Country yielded. With this prey he sailed over the Ocean sea unto the Island of Hispaniola, and arrived first at the port of Isabella: and there he had reasonable [sale] of his English commodities, as also of some part of his Negroes . . . for which he received . . . such quantity of merchandise, that he did not only lade his own three ships with hides, ginger, sugars, and some quantity of pearls, but he [also filled two more] with hides and other like commodities, which he sent to Spain.” 

>-Source: Richard Hakluyt, *The Principal Navigations, Voyages, and Discoveries of the English Nation,* 1589

**The excerpt best serves as evidence for which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the triangular trade
the Great Awakening
the domestic slave trade
the Navigation Acts

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“[Hawkins] passed to Sierra Leone, upon the coast of Guinea . . . where he stayed some good time, and got into his possession, partly by the sword and partly by other means, to the number of 300 negroes at the least, besides other merchandises, which that Country yielded. With this prey he sailed over the Ocean sea unto the Island of Hispaniola, and arrived first at the port of Isabella: and there he had reasonable [sale] of his English commodities, as also of some part of his Negroes . . . for which he received . . . such quantity of merchandise, that he did not only lade his own three ships with hides, ginger, sugars, and some quantity of pearls, but he [also filled two more] with hides and other like commodities, which he sent to Spain.” 

>-Source: Richard Hakluyt, *The Principal Navigations, Voyages, and Discoveries of the English Nation,* 1589

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the development of an Atlantic economy exchanging goods and enslaved people
the cruelty practiced by the Spanish colonial government, known as the Black Legend
the decline in transatlantic piracy following the construction of the Spanish armada
the emergence of slave trading posts on the West Coast of Africa in the late 1600s

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“Mercantilism provided the ideas that governed colonial economic relations. These were that colonial interests were subordinate to the mother country, that trade with and of its colonies should be restricted to English subjects, that the surplus products of a colony should be sent to the mother country, and that the trade and resources of a colony should be kept out of the hands of rivals. Colonies were to provide a captive market for English manufactured goods. Monopolizing trade with its colonies could stimulate domestic employment and industry, thereby reducing industrial unrest, poverty, and idleness.”

>-Source: Alvin Rabushka, political scientist, *Taxation in Colonial America,* 2008

**The ideas described in the excerpt contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the passage of laws restricting trade by and with the American colonies
the development of a robust manufacturing sector in the American colonies
the emigration of religious dissidents from Europe to North America
the emergence of an antitrust movement protesting business monopolies

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below. 

>“Mercantilism provided the ideas that governed colonial economic relations. These were that colonial interests were subordinate to the mother country, that trade with and of its colonies should be restricted to English subjects, that the surplus products of a colony should be sent to the mother country, and that the trade and resources of a colony should be kept out of the hands of rivals. Colonies were to provide a captive market for English manufactured goods. Monopolizing trade with its colonies could stimulate domestic employment and industry, thereby reducing industrial unrest, poverty, and idleness.”

>-Source: Alvin Rabushka, political scientist, *Taxation in Colonial America,* 2008

**Which of the following best supports the general argument in the excerpt about how mercantilism governed colonial economic relations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the Navigation Acts
the consumer revolution
the Stono Rebellion
the Enlightenment

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“As the contagion caused a great many Hurons to die, these people, not recognizing therein the justice of God, who takes vengeance for their crimes, imagined that the French were the cause of their death. . . . 

>Upon the strength of these reports . . . these barbarians have made attempts upon the lives of our Fathers, even going so far as to talk in open council of slaying them, but . . . on hearing this talk [a Native American leader named Taratouan] drew out a long string of porcelain, and threw it down in the midst of the assembly, saying, “There is something to close your mouths and stop your talking.” 

>-Source: Father Paul le Jeune, *Relation of What Occurred in New France in the Year 1636*

**The event described in the excerpt is an example of which of the following developments in colonial North America?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how trade with Europeans led to the spread of disease in indigenous communities
how French priests facilitated the creation of a vast trading network in North America
how indigenous communities adopted the rhetoric of republican government to protest European invasion
how European trade goods led to violent conflict between indigenous communities

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“As the contagion caused a great many Hurons to die, these people, not recognizing therein the justice of God, who takes vengeance for their crimes, imagined that the French were the cause of their death. . . . 

>Upon the strength of these reports . . . these barbarians have made attempts upon the lives of our Fathers, even going so far as to talk in open council of slaying them, but . . . on hearing this talk [a Native American leader named Taratouan] drew out a long string of porcelain, and threw it down in the midst of the assembly, saying, “There is something to close your mouths and stop your talking.” 

>-Source: Father Paul le Jeune, *Relation of What Occurred in New France in the Year 1636*

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the conflict referred to in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: trade relationships
cultural misunderstandings
racial discrimination
political disagreements

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“We had more guns and iron-headed arrows than before, but our enemies the Snake Indians and their allies had horses on which they rode, swift as the deer, on which they dashed at the Piegans, and with their stone clubs knocked them on the head, and had thus lost several of their best men. This news we did not well comprehend and it alarmed us, for we had no idea of horses and could not make out what they were. . . . 

>Next morning at the dawn of day, we attacked the tents, and with our sharp flat daggers and knives, cut through the tents and entered for the fight; but our war whoop instantly stopped, our eyes were appalled with terror; there was no one to fight with but the dead and the dying, each a mass of corruption. . . . The second day after, this dreadful disease broke out in our camp . . .”

>-Source: Saukamappee, from the Cree nation, "On the Arrival of Guns, Horses, and Smallpox in the Plains in the 1700s"

**Which of the following developments contributed most directly to the conditions described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increasing trade between indigenous nations and Europeans
decreasing indigenous exposure to European diseases
declining fertility rates among indigenous women
emerging alliances between previously hostile indigenous nations

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below.

>“We had more guns and iron-headed arrows than before, but our enemies the Snake Indians and their allies had horses on which they rode, swift as the deer, on which they dashed at the Piegans, and with their stone clubs knocked them on the head, and had thus lost several of their best men. This news we did not well comprehend and it alarmed us, for we had no idea of horses and could not make out what they were. . . . 

>Next morning at the dawn of day, we attacked the tents, and with our sharp flat daggers and knives, cut through the tents and entered for the fight; but our war whoop instantly stopped, our eyes were appalled with terror; there was no one to fight with but the dead and the dying, each a mass of corruption. . . . The second day after, this dreadful disease broke out in our camp . . .”

>-Source: Saukamappee, from the Cree nation, "On the Arrival of Guns, Horses, and Smallpox in the Plains in the 1700s"

**The views expressed in the excerpt are best seen as evidence of which of the following in Native American societies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how the introduction of European trade goods led to cultural changes
how warfare on the Plains remained unchanged after the arrival of Europeans
how the spread of disease led to conflict between indigenous groups
how indigenous gender roles were more egalitarian than those of European societies

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>“The metropolitan efforts to complete this British imperial hierarchy at the centre and to export it abroad, so as to forge ‘an entire interactive system,' were never a complete success [and] the American colonists eventually rejected the whole thing outright . . . [The British imperial] hierarchical world-view that was extended across the British empire was conservative in its ideology, and stressed the importance of tradition and unchangingness . . . the elaborate, layered social ordering of the American colonies was, at the mid-eighteenth century, a relatively recent development.” 

>-Source: David Cannadine, historian, *Ornamentalism: How the British Saw Their Empire,* 2002

**Which of the following most likely contributed to the ‘efforts to complete this British imperial hierarchy’ referred to in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the economic theory of mercantilism
the establishment of Anglican churches
the emergence of an American upper class
the transatlantic slave trade

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question below.

>“The metropolitan efforts to complete this British imperial hierarchy at the centre and to export it abroad, so as to forge ‘an entire interactive system,' were never a complete success [and] the American colonists eventually rejected the whole thing outright . . . [The British imperial] hierarchical world-view that was extended across the British empire was conservative in its ideology, and stressed the importance of tradition and unchangingness . . . the elaborate, layered social ordering of the American colonies was, at the mid-eighteenth century, a relatively recent development.” 

>-Source: David Cannadine, historian, *Ornamentalism: How the British Saw Their Empire,* 2002

**Which of the following evidence would best support the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The British government created a coherent, hierarchical structure in its colonies to pursue its mercantilist economic aims.
The British government strictly enforced its policies to fully control the methods of production in the American colonies.
The British government created a unique parliamentary structure in the colonies that used the system of virtual representation
The British government created an imperial structure of government similar to the encomienda system in colonial Spain

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“. . . from thence forward, no goods or commodities whatsoever shall be imported into or exported out of any lands . . . in any other . . . ships or . . . vessels whatsoever, but in such ships or vessels as do truly and without fraud belong only to the people of England . . . or are built of and belonging to any of the lands and whereof the master and three fourths of the mariners at least are English.” 

>-Source: *Navigation Acts,* September 1660

**Which of the following accurately describes the response colonists had to the law above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: colonists began smuggling goods to trade with the Dutch
colonists began adding colonial stamps to all tea and paper products
colonial governments began boycotting British products in retaliation
colonial governments began taxing British imports in retaliation

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“. . . from thence forward, no goods or commodities whatsoever shall be imported into or exported out of any lands . . . in any other . . . ships or . . . vessels whatsoever, but in such ships or vessels as do truly and without fraud belong only to the people of England . . . or are built of and belonging to any of the lands and whereof the master and three fourths of the mariners at least are English.” 

>-Source: *Navigation Acts,* September 1660

**Which of the following contributed most directly to the enactment of the law in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the economic theory that colonies existed to enrich the mother country
the impressment of American sailors during the Napoleonic Wars
the expansion of the British navy during the reign of Queen Elizabeth I
the discovery of precious metals in the New England and Middle colonies
